slides.heading: ExpandableCardList
slides.body:
ExpandableCardList is useful when you want a compact set of ideas that reveal details one at a time. The input is an items array with id, icon, label, and reveal text, plus an optional multiOpen flag.

Example input: { type: "ExpandableCardList", multiOpen: false, items: [{ id: "state", icon: "box", label: "State", reveal: "State is data that can change over time." }] }
bullets: Best for glossary entries, conceptual checkpoints, and progressive disclosure.
Click a card to reveal its explanation.
Set multiOpen to true when comparing several revealed items matters.

renderConfig.hint: Click a card to reveal more detail.

items.label: State
items.reveal: Data that changes while the learner interacts with the lesson.

items.label: Event
items.reveal: A user action, command, or step that updates the model.

items.label: Feedback
items.reveal: The visual response that makes the concept feel concrete.

slides.heading: ClickRevealGrid
slides.body:
ClickRevealGrid shows several choices at once, then reveals a detail panel inside the selected tile. The input is an items array with id, icon, label, and detail, plus optional columns and detailLabel.

Example input: { type: "ClickRevealGrid", columns: 2, detailLabel: "Why it matters", items: [{ id: "props", icon: "data", label: "Props", detail: "Props configure the component." }] }
bullets: Best for categories, misconceptions, or pick-to-inspect moments.
Use columns to control density.
Each item can override the global detail label.

renderConfig.hint: Click a tile to inspect its detail.

items.label: Props
items.detail: Plain JSON values that configure what the component renders.

items.label: Interaction
items.detail: The learner clicks one tile and receives immediate local feedback.

items.label: Layout
items.detail: The columns value controls how many cards appear per row.

items.label: Detail
items.detail: The detail string appears only after the item is selected.

slides.heading: StepSimulator
slides.body:
StepSimulator is for processes that unfold over several hops. The input has actors and steps: actors define the nodes, steps define messages moving from one actor to another.

Example input: { type: "StepSimulator", actors: [{ icon: "user", label: "User", sublabel: "clicks", color: "...", border: "..." }], steps: [{ from: "User", to: "App", action: "click button" }] }
bullets: Best for request flows, lifecycle sequences, and cause-effect chains.
Actors stay visible while the step log grows.
Use isManager on an actor to highlight a coordinator.

slides.heading: ComparePanel
slides.body:
ComparePanel presents two sides that can be opened independently. The input has left and right objects with icon, title, colors, and optional revealContent or locked state.

Example input: { type: "ComparePanel", left: { title: "Before", locked: true }, right: { title: "After", revealContent: ["Clearer", "Faster"] } }
bullets: Best for before-after, open-closed, or wrong-right comparisons.
locked true creates a denied state instead of normal reveal content.
successMessage appears after the right side is opened.

renderConfig.hint: Click both sides to compare behavior.

left.title: Opaque UI
left.lockedMessage: No model is visible, so the learner has to guess.

renderConfig.successMessage: Use ComparePanel when the contrast is the lesson.

slides.heading: JourneyFlow
slides.body:
JourneyFlow renders a vertical sequence where each step has an action and result. The input is steps with icon, action, result, color, and border, plus optional storeIcon and labels.

Example input: { type: "JourneyFlow", steps: [{ icon: "file", action: "Write file", result: "File appears" }], storeIcon: "disk" }
bullets: Best for persistence, pipelines, and ordered transformations.
Each click advances one step.
The optional store marker anchors the idea of a persistent destination.

steps.result: The component gets plain config data.
Internal state records which step is active.
The learner sees the next outcome in sequence.

slides.heading: InteractiveBuckets
slides.body:
InteractiveBuckets lets learners move items between two named containers. The input has items, source bucket metadata, destination bucket metadata, and an optional destinationTip.

Example input: { type: "InteractiveBuckets", items: ["cache", "db"], source: { label: "Available" }, destination: { label: "Selected" } }
bullets: Best for classification, memory vs storage, staging, and selection.
Click source items to move them to the destination.
Click destination items to move them back.

renderConfig.hint: Move ideas into the selected bucket.
renderConfig.destinationTip: Buckets are good when movement is the mental model.

slides.heading: CollapsibleTree
slides.body:
CollapsibleTree renders a nested tree where folder nodes expand and file nodes sit at the leaves. The input is one root tree node with name, type, and optional children.

Example input: { type: "CollapsibleTree", tree: { name: "root", type: "folder", children: [{ name: "notes.md", type: "file" }] } }
bullets: Best for hierarchical concepts.
Each folder keeps its own expanded state.
Use indent to tune the visual depth.

renderConfig.hint: Click folders to expand or collapse.
renderConfig.tip: Folders can contain files or more folders.

slides.heading: FileNavigator
slides.body:
FileNavigator renders a breadcrumb-driven folder browser. The input is a nested object: each key is a name, null means file, and an object means folder.

Example input: { type: "FileNavigator", rootLabel: "project", tree: { src: { "index.ts": null }, "README.md": null } }
bullets: Best for teaching navigation and nested project structure.
Folders are clickable, files are not.
Breadcrumbs let the learner jump back up.

renderConfig.hint: Open folders and use the breadcrumb to go back.
renderConfig.tip: Object values are folders; null values are files.

slides.heading: InfoCallout
slides.body:
InfoCallout is a compact highlighted message. The input is text, optional icon, and variant. Variants include info, success, warning, error, and neutral.

Example input: { type: "InfoCallout", variant: "warning", icon: "!", text: "This step changes state." }
bullets: Best for tips, warnings, conclusions, and short guidance.
It is intentionally small and direct.
Use variant to match the tone of the message.

renderConfig.text: A small callout can make an important idea feel anchored without taking over the slide.

slides.heading: CommandTable
slides.body:
CommandTable renders command reference rows without relying on markdown table parsing. The input is rows with command, meaning, and optional note, plus optional column labels.

Example input: { type: "CommandTable", rows: [{ command: "git init", meaning: "Create the repository vault", note: "Run once" }] }
bullets: Best for CLI command references.
Keeps commands in monospace and descriptions in readable cells.
Use note when a command needs a warning or context.

rows.meaning: Create a hidden .git repository in the current folder.
Show changed, staged, and untracked files.

slides.heading: GitCommitGraph
slides.body:
GitCommitGraph renders commits grouped by branch lane. The input is commits with id, message, branch, optional HEAD flag, and optional detail; branches can provide line metadata.

Example input: { type: "GitCommitGraph", commits: [{ id: "a1b2", message: "init", branch: "main", isHead: true }] }
bullets: Best for Git history and branch mental models.
Click a commit to reveal its detail.
Use branch names to split commits into lanes.

renderConfig.hint: Click a commit to read its detail.
renderConfig.tip: Commit ids are shown as short labels on the graph.

commits.detail: The first snapshot creates a baseline.
HEAD points at the latest commit on main.
A separate branch can explore a different idea.

slides.heading: GitStagingArea
slides.body:
GitStagingArea shows the three Git zones: working directory, staging area, and repository. The input is files with name and status, plus optional interactive and tip.

Example input: { type: "GitStagingArea", interactive: true, files: [{ name: "app.ts", status: "modified" }] }
bullets: Best for explaining git add and git commit.
Interactive files move through zones when clicked.
Statuses map to the starting zone.

renderConfig.hint: Click files to move them through Git's zones.
renderConfig.tip: Working directory to staging area to repository.

slides.heading: SimulatedTerminal
slides.body:
SimulatedTerminal embeds the terminal UI as an illustration. The input can set language, hint, initialVfs, preHistory, and height.

Example input: { type: "SimulatedTerminal", language: "linux", preHistory: ["pwd", "ls"], height: 280 }
bullets: Best when the terminal itself is the object of study.
Use linux or git language modes.
For active quest work, prefer the real challenge terminal dock.

slides.heading: VisualWorld
slides.body:
VisualWorld renders a live filesystem-style view. The input can provide vfs and cwd directly, or it can read terminalState from the runtime when used beside the real terminal.

Example input: { type: "VisualWorld", cwd: ["home", "student"], vfs: { home: { type: "directory", children: { ... } } } }
bullets: Best for filesystem and Git workspace visualization.
In quest UI, Vis now means this component only.
When a slide does not require terminal, the Vis option stays hidden.

slides.heading: Multi
slides.body:
Multi stacks several illustration configs vertically. The input is an illustrations array and an optional gap. It is useful when one slide needs a callout plus a small interaction.

Example input: { type: "Multi", gap: 12, illustrations: [{ type: "InfoCallout", text: "First" }, { type: "ClickRevealGrid", items: [...] }] }
bullets: Best for composed slides.
Each child is a normal IllustrationConfig.
Keep the total height reasonable so the slide remains scannable.

illustrations.text: Multi lets one slide combine simple building blocks.

illustrations.hint: Click a tile in this nested child component.

items.label: Child A
items.detail: This is the first child config.

items.label: Child B
items.detail: This is the second child config.
